title: From models to materials
summary: This lesson explores the fundamental link between the microscopic world of chemical bonding and the macroscopic physical properties of materials. Students will learn to predict properties like melting point, conductivity, and solubility based on whether a substance has ionic, covalent, or metallic bonding. The lesson also introduces alloys and polymers as important examples of materials engineered for specific purposes.
learningObjectives: Deduce the type of bonding and structure from the physical properties of a substance.
Explain the properties of ionic compounds, simple covalent molecules, giant covalent structures, and metals in terms of their structure and bonding.
Compare the structure and properties of alloys with their constituent metals.
Distinguish between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers in terms of their structure and response to heat.

simpleExplanation.title: Building with Bonds
simpleExplanation.summary: The way atoms connect to each other determines the properties of the final material, like its strength or ability to conduct electricity. By understanding the type of bond, we can predict and explain how a substance will behave.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine building with different types of connectors. If you glue Lego bricks together (covalent bonds in a giant structure), you get a very strong, rigid object. If you just pile them loosely in a box (weak forces between simple molecules), they are easy to separate. If you use magnets (ionic bonds), they stick together strongly but can be shattered if hit from the wrong angle. If you have a box of ball bearings (metallic bonding), they stick together but can easily slide around, changing shape without breaking.
steps: Identify the bonding type: Determine if the substance is ionic (metal + non-metal), covalent (non-metals only), or metallic (metals only).
Describe the resulting structure: Is it a giant lattice (ionic, metallic, covalent) or simple discrete molecules?
Link structure to properties: Relate the structure to melting point, conductivity, and malleability based on the forces involved.
Justify your reasoning: Explain *why* the properties arise, referring to the energy needed to overcome forces or the presence of mobile charge carriers.

flashcards.front: What determines the physical properties of a substance?
flashcards.back: The type of chemical bonding (ionic, covalent, metallic) and the resulting structure (e.g., giant lattice, simple molecular).

flashcards.front: Why do ionic compounds have high melting points?
flashcards.back: They form giant ionic lattices with strong electrostatic forces of attraction between oppositely charged ions, which require a large amount of energy to overcome.
When a force is applied, layers of ions can shift, bringing ions with the same charge next to each other. The resulting repulsion shatters the crystal.

flashcards.front: When do ionic compounds conduct electricity?
flashcards.back: Only when molten or dissolved in a polar solvent (like water). In these states, the ions are mobile and can act as charge carriers.

flashcards.front: Distinguish between the forces that are overcome when melting a simple molecular substance versus a giant covalent substance.
flashcards.back: For simple molecular substances, weak intermolecular forces are overcome. For giant covalent structures, strong covalent bonds must be broken.

flashcards.front: Why is graphite an electrical conductor while diamond is not?
flashcards.back: Graphite has delocalised electrons between its layers that are free to move and carry charge. In diamond, all valence electrons are held in strong, localised covalent bonds.
A mixture of a metal with at least one other element, which can be a metal or non-metal.

flashcards.front: Why are alloys generally harder and stronger than pure metals?
flashcards.back: The different sized atoms in the alloy disrupt the regular lattice structure, making it more difficult for the layers of atoms to slide over one another.
A polymer with long chains held together by weak intermolecular forces. It can be melted and reshaped upon heating because these weak forces are easily overcome.
A polymer with strong covalent cross-links between its chains, forming a rigid 3D network. It does not melt upon heating and will char at high temperatures.
Volatility describes how easily a substance evaporates at normal temperatures. Substances with weak intermolecular forces, like simple molecular compounds, are typically more volatile.

faq.q: How can I tell if a covalent compound will have a simple molecular or giant covalent structure?
faq.a: You are expected to memorise the common examples of giant covalent structures: diamond (C), graphite (C), silicon (Si), and silicon dioxide (SiO₂). Most other covalent compounds you encounter in SL Chemistry, especially those involving combinations of non-metals like H, N, O, S, and halogens, will have simple molecular structures.

faq.q: Why is graphite soft and slippery if it's a giant covalent structure?
faq.a: Graphite has a layered structure. Within each layer, carbon atoms are strongly bonded covalently. However, between the layers, there are only weak intermolecular forces. These layers can easily slide over each other, which makes graphite feel soft and slippery, and useful as a lubricant.

faq.q: Are alloys considered compounds or mixtures?
faq.a: Alloys are best described as mixtures, specifically a type of mixture called a solid solution. This is because the constituent elements are not present in a fixed ratio by mass, unlike in a chemical compound. You can have different percentages of zinc in brass, for example.

sections.content: Welcome to the intersection of chemical theory and real-world application. In this lesson, we move from abstract models of atoms and bonds to understanding the materials that build our world. We will explore how the simple rules of bonding dictate whether a material is hard or soft, conductive or insulating, brittle or malleable. This knowledge is crucial for designing and selecting materials for everything from smartphone screens to aircraft bodies.
Structure, Bonding & Properties: The Core Relationship

The physical properties of a substance are a direct consequence of its bonding and structure. By identifying the type of bonding—ionic, covalent, or metallic—we can predict the arrangement of particles and, from there, explain its macroscopic behaviour. We will consider four main structural types: giant ionic lattices, simple molecular structures, giant covalent structures, and metallic lattices.

items: **Ionic Bonding** leads to **Giant Ionic Lattices** (e.g., NaCl).
**Covalent Bonding** leads to **Simple Molecules** (e.g., H₂O, CO₂) or **Giant Covalent Structures** (e.g., Diamond, SiO₂).
**Metallic Bonding** leads to **Giant Metallic Lattices** (e.g., Cu, Fe).

sections.content: Ionic compounds consist of a regular, repeating 3D arrangement of positive and negative ions called a giant ionic lattice. The ions are held together by very strong electrostatic forces of attraction. The strength of these forces dictates the compound's properties.

items: **High Melting/Boiling Points:** A large amount of thermal energy is needed to overcome the strong electrostatic forces and break down the lattice.
**Non-conductive in Solid State:** Ions are held in fixed positions and are not free to move as charge carriers.
**Conductive when Molten or Aqueous:** When melted or dissolved, the ions are mobile and can move to carry an electric current.
**Brittle:** Distortion of the crystal lattice brings ions with like charges into proximity, causing strong repulsion that shatters the crystal.

sections.question: Explain, with reference to structure and bonding, why magnesium oxide (MgO) has a significantly higher melting point than sodium chloride (NaCl).
sections.solution:
1. Both MgO and NaCl are ionic compounds and form giant ionic lattice structures. ✓
2. The ions in MgO are Mg²⁺ and O²⁻, while in NaCl they are Na⁺ and Cl⁻. ✓
3. The magnitude of the charges on the ions in MgO (+2 and -2) is greater than in NaCl (+1 and -1). ✓
4. This results in stronger electrostatic forces of attraction between the ions in the MgO lattice compared to the NaCl lattice. ✓
5. Therefore, more energy is required to overcome these stronger forces in MgO, leading to a higher melting point. ✓

sections.content: Covalent compounds present a crucial distinction. Some form simple, discrete molecules with weak forces between them, while others form vast networks of covalently bonded atoms. Their properties are dramatically different.

items: **Simple Molecular Structures (e.g., CO₂, I₂):** Low melting/boiling points because only weak intermolecular forces (van der Waals', dipole-dipole) need to be overcome. They do not conduct electricity as there are no mobile charged particles.

**Giant Covalent Structures (e.g., Diamond, SiO₂):** Very high melting/boiling points because strong covalent bonds must be broken throughout the structure, requiring a vast amount of energy. They are typically very hard and do not conduct electricity (exception: graphite).

sections.content: A very common exam mistake is to state that 'covalent bonds are broken' when explaining the low melting point of a simple molecular substance like methane. Be precise! It is the weak *intermolecular forces* between the molecules that are overcome, not the strong covalent bonds *within* the molecules.

Metallic bonding involves a lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a 'sea' of delocalised valence electrons. These mobile electrons are key to explaining the characteristic properties of metals. Alloys are mixtures containing metals, designed to enhance certain properties.

items: **Good Electrical/Thermal Conductors:** The delocalised electrons are free to move throughout the lattice and carry charge or transfer kinetic energy.
**Malleable and Ductile:** The layers of positive ions can slide over one another without breaking the metallic bond, as the delocalised electrons can move to accommodate the new arrangement.

**Alloys are Harder:** The presence of different sized atoms disrupts the regular layers, making it more difficult for them to slide. This increases the hardness and strength of the material (e.g., steel is harder than iron).

sections.content: Polymers are large molecules (macromolecules) composed of many repeating subunits, known as monomers. Their properties depend on how the long polymer chains interact with each other.

items: **Thermoplastics (e.g., poly(ethene)):** Consist of individual polymer chains held by weak intermolecular forces. They soften on heating as chains can slide past each other, and can be remoulded. This process is reversible.

**Thermosetting plastics (e.g., Bakelite):** Have strong covalent cross-links between the polymer chains, creating a rigid 3D network. They do not soften on heating and will decompose at very high temperatures. This process is irreversible.

sections.question: Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc. Explain why brass is harder than pure copper.
sections.solution:
1. In pure copper, the atoms are all the same size and are arranged in a regular lattice. ✓
2. This regular arrangement allows layers of copper atoms to slide over each other relatively easily, making copper malleable. ✓
3. Brass is an alloy, where zinc atoms (which are a different size to copper atoms) are introduced into the copper lattice. ✓
4. This disrupts the regular arrangement of the layers. ✓
5. As a result, it is more difficult for the layers of atoms to slide past one another, making the alloy harder than the pure metal. ✓

interactiveEmbed.hint: Explore “States of Matter” — drag sliders and watch how the model responds in real time.
